Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 703 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tarun Hotchand Chainani

Facts.Text: On 1999-11-16, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh incorporated Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd as equal shareholders.

On 2004-12-24, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh incorporated Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd and transferred their respective shares in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, remaining equal shareholders in Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
From 2004-12-24 onwards, Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd wholly owned Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
From 2004-12-24 onwards, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh were the only two directors of both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1999 to carry on general wholesale trade, in particular of electronic products and mobile phones.
From 1999-11-16, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh were business partners and equal owners of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
From 2004-12-24, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh were equal shareholders of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, which wholly owned Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

Around 2005-01-01, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh began using funds of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, on the basis that they would keep records of principal sums and profits and that profits from such investments would be shared equally between them through Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

From around 2005-01-01, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh used funds of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to invest in at least ten properties, treating those investments as being made for Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

In late 2012-12-01, a residential unit at 7 Siglap Road #06-55 Mandarin Gardens, Singapore 448909, held in the name of or for the benefit of Avinderpal Singh, was sold and sale proceeds of S$295,496.97 were received by him.
On 2013-09-28, Tarun Hotchand Chainani emailed Avinderpal Singh requesting a statement of accounts for the Mandarin Gardens investment and asking about the balance of the sale proceeds.

On 2013-09-28, Avinderpal Singh replied by email stating that about S$200,000 had been paid as property tax for Mandarin Gardens, that funds had been deposited in HSBC, and that he would check and revert.
On 2014-01-07, Tarun Hotchand Chainani emailed Avinderpal Singh seeking confirmation of his own calculation of the profit from the sale of Mandarin Gardens.
In 2014-11-01, a residential unit at 15 Evelyn Road #28-02, Singapore 309311, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.

On 2017-12-04, Avinderpal Singh sent Tarun Hotchand Chainani a spreadsheet purporting to set out figures for the purchase, expenses, and sale of the Evelyn Road property, including payments to Simrit Kaur Dang and to Myanma Food For Thought Pte Ltd.
In 2015-08-01, a unit at 626 Upper Thomson Road #01-33 Meadows @ Pierce, Singapore 787130, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.
In 2016-08-01, a unit at 53B Grange Road #16-01 Spring Grove, Singapore 249567, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.

On 2016-08-23, an email from Kristin Callang, acting on instructions from Avinderpal Singh, was sent to Tarun Hotchand Chainani listing payments made from the sale proceeds of the Spring Grove property, including transfers to Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, Myanma Food For Thought Pte Ltd, a HSBC credit card, Marina One Residences, a swimming club, a POSB account, and a CIMB transfer, and stating a remaining balance of S$71,022.
In 2017-01-01, a unit at 52 Flora Drive #08-09 Parc Olympia, Singapore 506869, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.
In 2017-12-01, a unit at 531 Bedok Reservoir Road #01-111 Archipelago, Singapore 479282, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.
In 2017-03-01, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent WhatsApp messages to Avinderpal Singh asking about the net equity in the Archipelago and Spottiswoode properties after repayment of their mortgages.
In 2019-07-01, a unit at 48 Spottiswoode Park Road #22-06 Spottiswoode Residences, Singapore 088660, associated with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.
In 2013-07-01, a Dubai property identified as Burj Vista Tower 1/3305, Dubai, associated with the investment activities funded from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold.

In 2014-01-01, several Dubai properties associated with the investment activities funded from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, including Mira V-V-156, The Hills B2-702, The Hills B2-1204, The Hills A2-1401, The Hills C1-5-505, BD Address FV-21-2105, BD Blvd Point 44-4405, and BD Blvd Point 39-3901, were sold over the course of that year.
On 2015-07-15, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent a WhatsApp message to Avinderpal Singh asking that his brother, Davinderpal Singh, provide accounts for the Dubai properties.
On 2016-02-12, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent a WhatsApp message to Avinderpal Singh requesting final accounts for the Dubai properties.
On 2016-06-12, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent a WhatsApp message to Avinderpal Singh asking him to bring back all Dubai property accounts, stating that they were long overdue.

On 2016-07-21, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent a WhatsApp message to Avinderpal Singh requesting that the Dubai accounts be emailed, and Avinderpal Singh replied that he was chasing his brother for them.

On 2016-08-19, Tarun Hotchand Chainani created a WhatsApp group named "DXB Accounts" with himself, Avinderpal Singh, and Davinderpal Singh, and requested a complete account for all Dubai property transactions by 2016-08-26.

On 2016-09-14, Kristin Callang emailed Tarun Hotchand Chainani a spreadsheet titled "DUBAI PROPERTY PAYMENT MADE FROM AVITAR" listing payments from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd for eight Dubai properties, including BD Blvd Point 39-3901.

On 2016-09-15, Tarun Hotchand Chainani sent a WhatsApp message to Avinderpal Singh stating that the Dubai spreadsheet showed only cash flows between Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avinderpal Singh and asking for complete statements of accounts for all properties purchased and sold, including details of proceeds and recipients.

On 2018-07-10, Avinderpal Singh sent Tarun Hotchand Chainani a spreadsheet setting out payments made in 2014, 2015, and 2016 for the purchase of units at 21 Marina Way #19-11 and #20-09, Singapore 018978.
On 2018-07-10, Avinderpal Singh sent Tarun Hotchand Chainani updated spreadsheets referring to eight Dubai properties, showing amounts paid and certain transfers to Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

On 2018-07-31, Avinderpal Singh sent Tarun Hotchand Chainani spreadsheets containing references to Marina Way properties, Spottiswoode, Archipelago, Spring Grove, Mandarin Gardens, Parc Olympia, Meadows @ Pierce, and certain share counters, with various line items for costs, loans, and transfers.

On 2009-11-19, the board of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, comprising Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh, recorded minutes resolving that an interim dividend of S$1.5 million for the year ending 2009-12-31 would be paid to its shareholder, Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
On 2009-11-20, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd issued a notice to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd stating that an interim dividend of S$1.5 million had been declared and was payable to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

Around 2005-01-01, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd issued 500,000 new shares at S$1 per share, increasing its total shares to 1,000,000, with the subscription amount of S$500,000 due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

In 2008-01-01, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd issued 1,000,000 new shares at S$1 per share, increasing its total shares to 2,000,000, with the subscription amount of S$1,000,000 due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

In financial statements for the year ending 2007-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from its holding company of approximately S$502,800, which included S$500,000 for the 2005 share subscription.
In financial statements for the year ending 2008-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from its holding company of approximately US$1,061,067.

On 2009-12-31, ledger entries in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded transfers of amounts due from directors, including an amount of US$450,935.10 from the director account of Tarun Hotchand Chainani and an amount of S$55,398 from the director account of Avinderpal Singh, to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

In financial statements for the year ending 2009-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd of approximately US$1,566,618 and reflected the declaration of a S$1.5 million dividend to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
In financial statements for the year ending 2009-12-31, Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd recorded receipt of a S$1.5 million dividend from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

On 2010-01-01, a journal entry in the ledger of Avinderpal Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded a transfer of the amount due from him as a director to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, bringing his director loan balance with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to zero for the year ending 2010-12-31.

In financial statements for the year ending 2010-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd recorded the S$1.5 million dividend and related intercompany balances between the two companies.

On 2011-01-01, a journal entry numbered 30409 in the ledger of Avinderpal Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded a credit of US$1,634,217.17 described as "AUDIT ENTRIES 2010", changing the cumulative balance in that ledger from a positive US$1,119,099.44 as at 2010-12-31 to a negative US$515,117.73.

By 2011-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd had transferred certain doubtful receivables from customers, totalling approximately US$607,678.10, into the director loan ledger of Avinderpal Singh and recorded a tax credit of about US$81,000 related to the write-off of those receivables.

From 2007-01-01 until the financial year ending 2015-12-31, Tarun Hotchand Chainani signed off annually on the financial statements of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd in his capacity as director.
From 2007-01-01 until at least 2015-12-31, Tarun Hotchand Chainani acted as a director and corporate secretary of both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

In 2017-01-01, Tarun Hotchand Chainani removed himself as a co-signatory from a Standard Chartered Bank account of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd after discussing a possible transfer of his shares in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to Avinderpal Singh.

In 2017-01-01, before removing himself as a co-signatory from the Standard Chartered Bank account of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, Tarun Hotchand Chainani informed Avinderpal Singh that no funds were to be removed from that account without his consent.

On 2015-12-14, a ledger of Avinderpal Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was prepared, covering transactions from 2008-06-30 to 2015-12-14, and included entries described as loans or payments between the company and him.

On 2019-04-12, an updated ledger of Avinderpal Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was prepared, covering transactions from 2008-06-30 to 2019-04-12, and included entries described as loans or payments between the company and him.
On 2021-07-26, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh entered into a settlement agreement that did not alter or limit the claims in Suit No 703 of 2020.

Up to the financial year ending 2015-12-31, amounts due from directors, including Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh, were recorded in the books of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and were annual audit by external accountants.

In 2020-08-01, after the commencement of Suit No 703 of 2020, Avinderpal Singh proposed that a law firm be appointed to represent Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd in the action, and Tarun Hotchand Chainani opposed this proposal.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the commencement of the dispute, the pattern of using Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd funds for property and share investments, combined with the handling of intercompany balances, director ledgers, and dividend declarations, created a situation in which the plaintiff’s economic interest as an equal ultimate owner of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd would be affected by how those investments and related cash flows were recorded, applied, and reported.
Party_Details.Rule: In shareholder disputes involving closely held companies, courts generally assess whether pre-dispute conduct in managing company assets and recording intercompany and director transactions aligns with the parties’ agreed investment arrangements and with company law requirements on share subscriptions, dividends, and maintenance of proper accounts, in order to determine if relief such as winding up or an order for accounts is appropriate.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute facts Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh jointly set up Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, used company funds for multiple property and share investments on an agreed basis, and recorded substantial intercompany and director balances, including share subscriptions, a S$1.5 million dividend, and a large audit journal entry affecting the director ledger of Avinderpal Singh. The plaintiff repeatedly requested detailed accounts for specific investments and for Dubai properties, and received partial spreadsheets and ledger extracts. These circumstances link the plaintiff’s position as an equal ultimate owner of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd to the way in which company funds were deployed into investments and how those transactions were documented and reconciled between the operating company, the holding company, and the directors’ ledgers.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court characterised the action as a shareholder oppression claim under s 216 of the Companies Act. It analysed whether the companies functioned as a quasi-partnership and concluded that, although that label applied to the relationship between the two individuals, the decisive element was the specific commercial understanding between them. The judge identified an informal understanding from around 2005 that company funds would be used to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of the company, that the investing individual would account for principal and profits to the company and to the other individual, and that profits would ultimately be shared equally through the holding company. The judge held that this understanding applied to all the listed Singapore and overseas properties and share counters, including BD Blvd Point 39-3901, and that the properties were to be treated as acquired on behalf of the company to the extent company funds were used.

On that basis, the judge concluded that Avinderpal Singh owed duties to account both to the company and to the plaintiff for principal sums and profits from those investments. The court found that, from about 2013 onwards, he persistently failed to provide proper accounts despite repeated requests, instead giving delayed, incomplete, or opaque spreadsheets. This conduct was treated as a clear and continuing failure to comply with the agreed accounting arrangement. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiff’s access to company records or the existence of some spreadsheets amounted to a discharge of the duty to account.

In assessing oppression, the court applied the standard of commercial unfairness under s 216, focusing on the parties’ legitimate expectations arising from their informal agreement. It held that the plaintiff, as a 50% shareholder of the holding company and ultimate owner of the operating company, was entitled to expect that investments made with company funds under the understanding would be properly accounted for. The persistent failure to account was found to be a visible departure from that expectation and therefore commercially unfair conduct in the affairs of the holding company, taking into account that the operating company was its sole asset. The court rejected the contention that only minority shareholders can invoke s 216, noting that a 50% shareholder without unilateral control can also seek relief.

The judge distinguished between personal oppression claims and corporate wrongs. Allegations that director loans or other payments recorded in the 2015 and 2019 ledgers were unauthorised, and that the large US$1.634 million audit entry and related bad-debt transfers were improper, were treated as potential wrongs to the company rather than to the plaintiff personally. Applying the reasoning in Suying Design and Sakae, the court held that any misapplication of company funds or misstatement of director balances would primarily injure the company, and that such matters should be pursued by or on behalf of the company, for example by a liquidator, rather than as oppression. The same analysis was applied to the S$1.5 million dividend: the court accepted the explanation that the dividend was declared to the holding company and set off against amounts due for share subscriptions and transferred director balances, and noted that the plaintiff had signed the relevant financial statements. It found no separate act of commercial unfairness to the plaintiff in relation to the dividend or the US$1.6 million entry.

Having found commercial unfairness only in relation to the failure to account for investments under the understanding, the court turned to remedies under s 216. It considered that both the holding company and the operating company were dormant and that the operating company functioned as a vehicle for the holding company and its two equal owners. Relying on the breadth of s 216(2) and (3) and on authorities such as Kumagai Gumi and Ng Kek Wee, the judge concluded that winding up both companies was an appropriate way to bring the unfairness to an end and to allow a neutral officeholder to deal with any corporate claims, including possible misapplication of funds or director loans.

The court ordered that both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd be wound up and indicated that liquidators would be appointed, with consequential directions to follow. It further ordered that an account be taken by Avinderpal Singh of all principal sums of company funds used to acquire stocks and real estate under the understanding and of all profits from those investments. The judge rejected attempts to exclude particular properties or shares from the scope of the account at this stage, holding that such objections should be addressed in the accounting process. The court declined to treat the director loan ledgers, the US$1.6 million audit entry, and the S$1.5 million dividend as separate oppression grounds, leaving any remedies for those matters to be pursued as corporate claims in the winding-up process. Overall, the plaintiff’s oppression claim was allowed to the extent of the findings on the understanding, the failure to account, and the grant of winding-up and accounting relief.

Party_Details.Name: Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh

Facts.Text: On 1999-11-16, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani incorporated Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd as equal shareholders.

On 2004-12-24, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani incorporated Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd and transferred their respective shares in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, remaining equal shareholders in Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
From 1999-11-16 onwards, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh was a director of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
From 2004-12-24 onwards, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh was a director of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
From 1999-11-16, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani were equal owners and co-directors of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
From 2004-12-24, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani were equal shareholders and co-directors of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, which wholly owned Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

Around 2005-01-01, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani began using funds of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, on the basis that they would keep records of principal sums and profits and that profits from such investments would be shared equally between them through Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

From around 2005-01-01, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh used funds of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to acquire multiple properties in Singapore and overseas, and certain listed shares, in his own name or in the names of nominees, treating those acquisitions as investments for Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

In late 2012-12-01, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh received S$295,496.97 as sale proceeds from the sale of a residential unit at 7 Siglap Road #06-55 Mandarin Gardens, Singapore 448909, which had been acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

On 2013-09-28, in response to an email from Tarun Hotchand Chainani about the Mandarin Gardens investment, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh stated by email that about S$200,000 had been paid as property tax, that funds had been deposited in HSBC, and that he would check and revert.

In 2014-11-01, a residential unit at 15 Evelyn Road #28-02, Singapore 309311, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

On 2017-12-04, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh sent a spreadsheet to Tarun Hotchand Chainani setting out figures for the purchase, expenses, and sale of the Evelyn Road property, including payments to Simrit Kaur Dang and to Myanma Food For Thought Pte Ltd.

In 2015-08-01, a unit at 626 Upper Thomson Road #01-33 Meadows @ Pierce, Singapore 787130, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

In 2016-08-01, a unit at 53B Grange Road #16-01 Spring Grove, Singapore 249567, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

On 2016-08-23, an email from Kristin Callang, acting on instructions from Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh, was sent to Tarun Hotchand Chainani listing payments made from the sale proceeds of the Spring Grove property, including transfers to Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, Myanma Food For Thought Pte Ltd, a HSBC credit card, Marina One Residences, a swimming club, a POSB account, and a CIMB transfer, and stating a remaining balance of S$71,022.

In 2017-01-01, a unit at 52 Flora Drive #08-09 Parc Olympia, Singapore 506869, acquired in connection with the investment activities of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

In 2017-12-01, a unit at 531 Bedok Reservoir Road #01-111 Archipelago, Singapore 479282, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

In 2019-07-01, a unit at 48 Spottiswoode Park Road #22-06 Spottiswoode Residences, Singapore 088660, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

In 2013-07-01, a Dubai property identified as Burj Vista Tower 1/3305, Dubai, acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, was sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

In 2014-01-01, several Dubai properties acquired using funds from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, including Mira V-V-156, The Hills B2-702, The Hills B2-1204, The Hills A2-1401, The Hills C1-5-505, BD Address FV-21-2105, BD Blvd Point 44-4405, and BD Blvd Point 39-3901, were sold and the proceeds were handled by or under the direction of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh.

On 2015-07-15, after Tarun Hotchand Chainani requested that Dubai property accounts be provided, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh replied by WhatsApp that he would arrange for his brother to provide them.

On 2016-07-21, after a further request from Tarun Hotchand Chainani for Dubai property accounts, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh replied by WhatsApp that he was chasing his brother for the information.

On 2016-09-14, Kristin Callang, on instructions from Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh, emailed a spreadsheet titled "DUBAI PROPERTY PAYMENT MADE FROM AVITAR" to Tarun Hotchand Chainani, listing payments from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd for eight Dubai properties.
On 2016-09-15, after receiving the Dubai spreadsheet, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh replied to a WhatsApp message from Tarun Hotchand Chainani stating that he would check when he returned.

On 2018-07-10, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh sent updated spreadsheets to Tarun Hotchand Chainani referring to eight Dubai properties and showing amounts paid and certain transfers to Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

On 2018-07-10, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh sent a spreadsheet to Tarun Hotchand Chainani setting out payments made in 2014, 2015, and 2016 for the purchase of units at 21 Marina Way #19-11 and #20-09, Singapore 018978.

By 2018-01-01, the Dubai properties Mira V-V-156, The Hills B2-702, The Hills B2-1204, The Hills A2-1401, The Hills C1-5-505, BD Address FV-21-2105, BD Blvd Point 44-4405, BD Blvd Point 39-3901, and Burj Vista Tower 1/3305 had been sold, while the Marina Way units remained unsold.

On 2009-11-19, as a director of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh participated in a board meeting that resolved to declare an interim dividend of S$1.5 million for the year ending 2009-12-31 to be paid to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 2009-11-20, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh signed a notice on behalf of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd stating that an interim dividend of S$1.5 million had been declared and was payable to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
Around 2005-01-01, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd issued 500,000 new shares at S$1 per share, and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd became to pay S$500,000 for those shares.
In 2008-01-01, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd issued 1,000,000 new shares at S$1 per share, and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd became to pay S$1,000,000 for those shares.

In financial statements for the year ending 2007-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd of approximately S$502,800, which included S$500,000 for the 2005 share subscription.
In financial statements for the year ending 2008-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd of approximately US$1,061,067.

On 2009-12-31, ledger entries in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded transfers of amounts due from directors, including US$450,935.10 from the director account of Tarun Hotchand Chainani and S$55,398 from the director account of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh, to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, increasing the amount due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd to Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

In financial statements for the year ending 2009-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded an amount due from Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd of approximately US$1,566,618 and reflected the declaration of a S$1.5 million dividend to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.

In financial statements for the year ending 2009-12-31, Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd recorded receipt of a S$1.5 million dividend from Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd, and these financial statements were signed by Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh as a director.

On 2010-01-01, a journal entry in the director loan ledger of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded a transfer of the amount due from him as a director to Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, bringing his director loan balance with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to zero for the year ending 2010-12-31.

In financial statements for the year ending 2010-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd recorded the S$1.5 million dividend and related intercompany balances, and these financial statements were signed by Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh as a director.

On 2011-01-01, a journal entry numbered 30409 in the director loan ledger of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd recorded a credit of US$1,634,217.17 described as "AUDIT ENTRIES 2010", changing the cumulative balance in that ledger from a positive US$1,119,099.44 as at 2010-12-31 to a negative US$515,117.73.

By 2011-12-31, Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd had transferred certain doubtful receivables from customers, totalling approximately US$607,678.10, into the director loan ledger of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and recorded a tax credit of about US$81,000 related to the write-off of those receivables, resulting in a net reduction of US$515,117.73 in his director loan balance.

On 2015-12-14, a ledger of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was prepared, covering transactions from 2008-06-30 to 2015-12-14, and included entries described as loans or payments between the company and him.

On 2019-04-12, an updated ledger of Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh with Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was prepared, covering transactions from 2008-06-30 to 2019-04-12, and included entries described as loans or payments between the company and him.

From 2007-01-01 until the financial year ending 2015-12-31, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh signed off annually on the financial statements of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd in his capacity as director.

In 2020-08-01, after the commencement of Suit No 703 of 2020, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh proposed that a law firm be appointed to represent Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd in the action.
On 2021-07-26, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani entered into a settlement agreement that did not alter or limit the claims in Suit No 703 of 2020.

Up to the financial year ending 2015-12-31, amounts due from directors, including Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani, were recorded in the books of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and were annual audit by external accountants.

In 2017-01-01, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh continued to operate bank accounts of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd after Tarun Hotchand Chainani removed himself as a co-signatory from a Standard Chartered Bank account, following discussions about a possible transfer of shares.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the commencement of the dispute, Avinderpal Singh’s handling of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd’s funds for property and share investments, his management of director loan ledgers and intercompany balances, and his responses to repeated requests for detailed accounts created a conflict between his role in managing company funds and the equal economic position of the other ultimate owner.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes involving closely held companies, a director who manages company funds and undertakes investments on behalf of the company is generally maintain accurate records, explain significant ledger entries and intercompany transfers, and provide coherent accounts of investment-related cash flows, so that the economic interests of all owners are properly reflected and can be verified.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh controlled or directed the use of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd funds for multiple property and share investments, including Singapore and Dubai properties and listed shares, and that he was centrally involved in recording director loans, intercompany balances, and a large audit journal entry affecting his director ledger. He participated in share issuances and a S$1.5 million dividend declaration that were used to adjust balances between the operating company, the holding company, and the directors. Over several years, the other equal owner requested detailed accounts for specific investments and for Dubai properties, and Avinderpal Singh responded with partial spreadsheets and explanations, while some transactions, such as the US$1.634 million audit entry and the treatment of doubtful receivables, were only explained later in the litigation. These circumstances frame the later judicial assessment of whether his conduct in managing and documenting company funds and investments was consistent with the expectations arising from the parties’ investment arrangement and their equal ownership structure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court accepted that Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and the plaintiff had an informal understanding from around 2005 that company funds would be used to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of the company, that the investing individual would account for principal and profits to the company and to the other individual, and that profits would ultimately be shared equally through the holding company. The judge held that this understanding applied to all the listed properties and share counters, including BD Blvd Point 39-3901, and that, to the extent company funds were used, the assets were to be treated as held on behalf of the company. On that basis, the court concluded that Avinderpal Singh owed duties to account both to the company and to the plaintiff for principal sums and profits from those investments.

Reviewing the chronology, the judge found that from about 2013 onwards Avinderpal Singh persistently failed to provide proper accounts despite repeated requests. The court noted that he delayed in providing information after sales of properties such as Mandarin Gardens, Evelyn Road, Meadows @ Pierce, Spring Grove, Parc Olympia, Archipelago, Spottiswoode, and various Dubai properties. When he did respond, he often supplied spreadsheets that lacked clear linkage between purchase prices, sale prices, expenses, and transfers back to the company, and that were unsupported by underlying documents. The judge considered that these spreadsheets did not amount to proper accounts under the agreed arrangement. The court also observed that more structured explanations, including for the Dubai properties and Marina Way units, appeared only in his affidavit of evidence-in-chief filed in 2023, long after the investments and after the dispute had arisen.

The judge rejected Avinderpal Singh’s argument that the plaintiff’s access to company records or the existence of some spreadsheets discharged his duty to account. The court emphasised that, under the understanding, the individual who conducted the investments was expected to provide coherent accounts, not merely leave the other owner to reconstruct transactions from raw records. The judge also rejected the suggestion that the understanding did not apply to certain properties or shares, holding that his admissions in pleadings and submissions bound him to treat all the listed properties and shares as falling within the arrangement.

In assessing oppression, the court applied the commercial unfairness standard under s 216, focusing on the legitimate expectations arising from the understanding. It held that the plaintiff, as an equal shareholder of the holding company and ultimate owner of the operating company, was entitled to expect that investments made with company funds under the understanding would be properly accounted for. The persistent failure by Avinderpal Singh to provide adequate accounts was found to be a clear departure from that expectation and therefore commercially unfair conduct in the affairs of the holding company. The court rejected his contention that only minority shareholders can invoke s 216, noting that a 50% shareholder without unilateral control can also seek relief.

The judge distinguished these findings from allegations about director loans, the US$1.634 million audit entry, and the S$1.5 million dividend. For those matters, the court accepted that Avinderpal Singh had provided an explanation: director balances were transferred from the operating company to the holding company, doubtful receivables were moved into his director ledger and written off, and the dividend was declared and set off against amounts due for share subscriptions and transferred director balances. The court held that any impropriety in those transactions would primarily constitute a wrong to the company rather than to the plaintiff personally, and therefore should be addressed as corporate claims, for example by a liquidator, rather than as oppression under s 216.

Having found that Avinderpal Singh’s failure to account for investments under the understanding amounted to commercial unfairness, the court considered appropriate relief. It noted that both the holding company and the operating company were dormant and that the operating company functioned as a vehicle for the holding company and its two equal owners. Relying on the broad remedial powers under s 216 and on authorities such as Kumagai Gumi and Ng Kek Wee, the judge ordered that both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd be wound up, with liquidators to be appointed and consequential directions to follow. The court also ordered that an account be taken by Avinderpal Singh of all principal sums of company funds used to acquire stocks and real estate under the understanding and of all profits from those investments. It declined to exclude particular properties or shares from the scope of the account at this stage, holding that any disputes about whether company funds were used should be resolved in the accounting process. The court did not grant oppression relief in relation to the director loan ledgers, the US$1.6 million audit entry, or the S$1.5 million dividend, leaving any remedies for those matters to be pursued as corporate claims in the winding-up process. On this basis, the court held Avinderpal Singh liable in the oppression action and granted winding-up and accounting relief against him.
